1.synthesis: 'Thus says the Lord, the God of the Hebrews, "Let my people go, that they may serve me. For if you refuse to let them go and still hold them, behold, the hand of the Lord will fall with a very severe plague upon your livestock that are in the field, the horses, the donkeys, the camels, the herds, and the flocks"' (vv.1–3). God appoints a fixed time — 'tomorrow' (v.5) — so that when the murrain strikes all Egyptian cattle while not one Israelite animal dies, Pharaoh cannot attribute it to natural causes. He sends to investigate and finds Israel's cattle untouched, yet 'the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he did not let the people go' (v.7).
1.historical_context: The plague directly assaulted Egypt's religious economy. The Apis bull — incarnation of the god Ptah — was among the most sacred objects in all Egypt; Hathor, the cow goddess, pervaded Egyptian iconography; and cattle formed the backbone of agricultural wealth in the Nile Delta. Adam Clarke notes that the murrain (deber) is a general term for any fatal pestilence, describing symptoms of severe systemic inflammation. Israel's cattle in Goshen breathed the same air and drank the same water, yet not one died — a geographically and scientifically verifiable distinction that Pharaoh himself confirms by sending to investigate. Keil and Delitzsch note that the appointed day distinguishes this from ordinary murrains, which occur intermittently in Egypt, making God's direct hand unmistakable.
1.literary_structure: The fifth plague follows the full warning-structure: divine command → confrontation of Pharaoh → specific threat → time appointed → immediate execution → report → heart hardened. The verb 'sever' (hiflah) that God uses in verse 4 denotes a marvellous, supernatural distinction — the same root used in Exodus 8:22 for the Goshen exemption from flies. The narrative's brief notation that 'all the cattle of Egypt died' (v.6) is immediately qualified by 'of the cattle of the children of Israel died not one' (v.6): the contrast is the entire point. Pharaoh's investigation (v.7) creates an ironic inversion — he confirms the miracle precisely and hardens his heart against it.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry on the economic meaning: 'They had made Israel poor, and now God would make them poor.' He adds the moral: 'What we make an idol of it is just with God to remove from us.' The Egyptians had made gods of their cattle; now the cattle die by the very hand of the God the Egyptians denied. Gill on the divine 'severing': 'the Lord shall sever — a marvellous separation; though the cattle of Israel breathed in the same air, drank of the same water, and fed in the same pastures, they had not the murrain.' Adam Clarke identifies the 'Lord' and 'God' titles in verse 1 (Yehovah and Elohey) as emphasizing the eternal power and Godhead of the one who speaks — contrasted with the impotence of Egypt's cow-deities. John Wesley: 'Doth God take care for oxen? Yes, he doth; his providence extends itself to the meanest of his creatures.'
1.christology: The divine protection of Israel's livestock — set in absolute contrast to Egypt's catastrophic losses — prefigures the shepherd's particular care for his own flock. Jesus declares: 'I know my own and my own know me' (John 10:14); 'I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand' (John 10:28). Not one Israelite animal dies while Egyptian cattle perish indiscriminately — the distinction drawn by God in Goshen is the precise pattern of the shepherd who 'calls his own sheep by name' (John 10:3) and preserves them through the very plague that destroys those outside his fold.

8.synthesis: With no warning given, God commands Moses and Aaron to take handfuls of ashes from a furnace and scatter them toward heaven in Pharaoh's sight: 'and it shall become fine dust over all the land of Egypt, and become boils breaking out in sores on man and beast throughout all the land of Egypt' (v.9). The plague falls immediately. For the first time the magicians 'could not stand before Moses because of the boils, for the boil was upon the magicians and upon all the Egyptians' (v.11), and they are driven from the field. 'But the Lord hardened the heart of Pharaoh, and he did not listen to them' (v.12).
8.historical_context: Keil and Delitzsch identify the Egyptian skin disease with the Hamm el Nil, the 'heat of the inundation' — a rash appearing at the close of the Nile's flood season, producing fiery, pustular eruptions. This gives the plague a natural substratum that God intensified supernaturally in its timing, universality, and severity. Charles Ellicott records the note of Havernick connecting the furnace-ash rite with an Egyptian expiation ceremony described by Manetho, in which ashes were winnowed — a sacred rite that God inverts into an instrument of pollution. Adam Clarke notes that the shechin (boil) and ababuoth (blisters) together describe a condition of inflammatory ulceration that ancient writers called the 'botch of Egypt' (Deuteronomy 28:27), a disease unknown before this plague.
8.literary_structure: The sixth plague departs structurally from its predecessors in two ways: no prior warning is given to Pharaoh, and both Moses and Aaron are sent together to perform the sign. The absence of warning signals an escalation — the space for repentance is narrowing. The symbolic weight of furnace ashes is pointed: the very furnaces in which Israelite labor was cruelly exploited (Exodus 1:14) now produce the instrument of Egypt's affliction. The defeat of the magicians (v.11) is the climax of their subplot: they had imitated the early plagues, confessed God's finger in the third, been unable to duplicate the fourth, and now are themselves struck by the sixth — their folly 'made manifest to all men' (2 Timothy 3:9).
8.theological_application: Matthew Henry captures the escalating principle: 'When they were not wrought upon by the death of their cattle, God sent a plague that seized their own bodies, and touched them to the quick. If less judgments do not do their work, God will send greater.' He also notes the symbolic justice: 'Sometimes God shows men their sin in their punishment; they had oppressed Israel in the furnaces, and now the ashes of the furnace are made as much a terror to them.' Gill on the magicians' defeat: 'They were righteously punished with boils for helping to harden Pharaoh's heart... for their contempt of the messengers and miracles of God.' John Wesley: 'Wilful hardness is commonly punished with judicial hardness. If men shut their eyes against the light, it is just with God to close their eyes. Let us dread this as the sorest judgment a man can be under on this side hell.'
8.christology: The boils breaking out from furnace ashes recall Job's affliction (Keil and Delitzsch: 'inflammations, like Job's'), and Job's suffering points forward to the suffering servant of Isaiah 53:4–5: 'he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows... with his wounds we are healed.' The furnace soot that produces Egyptian suffering is a typological reversal of the cross: what was an instrument of oppression becomes the occasion for judgment on the oppressor. The magicians 'could not stand before Moses' — just as Satan and his agents ultimately cannot stand before the one who bears the divine commission and through whom God's judicial purposes run their full course (Romans 8:33).

13.synthesis: God's longest speech to Pharaoh grounds the entire contest in theology: 'For this time I will send all my plagues on you yourself, and on your servants and your people, so that you may know that there is none like me in all the earth. For by now I could have put out my hand and struck you and your people with pestilence, and you would have been cut off from the earth. But for this purpose I have raised you up, to show you my power, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth' (vv.14–16). A grace is offered — those who fear the word of the Lord shelter their livestock (vv.19–21); the hail falls devastating man, beast, and plant throughout Egypt, sparing only Goshen (vv.22–26). Pharaoh confesses: 'This time I have sinned; the Lord is in the right, and I and my people are in the wrong' (v.27). Moses intercedes, the storm ceases, and Pharaoh hardens again (vv.28–35).
13.historical_context: Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott note that verse 15's 'I will stretch out my hand' should be read as past conditional: 'If I had stretched out my hand to smite you with pestilence, you would already have been cut off.' The verse describes a restraint exercised in previous plagues, not a new threat. The distinction between God-fearing and God-ignoring Egyptians in verses 19–21 is historically significant: it shows that the plague-sequence was producing a differentiated response even within Egypt — anticipating the 'mixed multitude' who would later depart with Israel (Exodus 12:38). Matthew Henry notes: 'God not only distinguished between Egyptians and Israelites, but between some Egyptians and others.' The theological climax is Paul's citation of verse 16 in Romans 9:17 as the paradigm case of divine sovereignty in history.
13.literary_structure: The seventh plague is the theological hinge of the plague cycle. It opens with the longest divine speech yet (vv.13–17), which explicitly states what the plagues have been accomplishing: the proclamation of God's name throughout all the earth. The humanitarian offer (vv.18–21) creates a moral distinction within Egypt itself: those who fear God's word act; those who do not, lose their livestock and servants in the field. The hail-storm narrative (vv.22–26) emphasizes Goshen's exemption. The repentance scene (vv.27–28) is the most theologically precise of Pharaoh's confessions: 'Jehovah is the righteous one; I and my people are the wicked ones.' But the relapse (vv.34–35) shows that external acknowledgment of divine justice without inward submission is worthless.
13.theological_application: Calvin on 'For this purpose I have raised you up' (v.16): 'God did not immediately destroy Pharaoh, but preserved him to stand for a time, because before he was born he had been predestinated for this purpose — that God might be glorified in his resistance.' Paul draws precisely this conclusion in Romans 9:17. Matthew Henry: 'Providence ordered it so that Moses should have a man of such a fierce and stubborn spirit to deal with, and every thing was so managed as to make it a most signal instance of the power God has to humble and bring down the proudest of his enemies.' Pharaoh's confession ('the Lord is righteous, I have sinned') is counterfeit repentance: Matthew Henry notes he 'deprecates the plague of locusts, not the plague of a hard heart, which was much the more dangerous.' The wheat and rye spared by the hail (vv.31–32) because they had not yet grown teaches that God's mercies even in judgment preserve the possibility of later fruitfulness.
13.christology: The divine speech of verses 14–17 is the Exodus's clearest statement that God's entire purpose in the plague-contest is the global proclamation of his name — which the New Testament identifies as the name of Jesus Christ, before whom 'every knee shall bow' (Philippians 2:10). The hail that levels everything in Egypt while Goshen stands untouched is a physical enactment of the judgment that falls on those outside the shelter of God's covering. Pharaoh's momentary confession that Jehovah is righteous, followed by his relapse, pictures the difference between the convicted conscience that acknowledges God under pressure and the reborn heart that confesses Christ unto salvation — the one is Esau's tears, the other is Jacob's wrestling (Hebrews 12:17).
